# Shell and TotalEnergies Paid to Protect Peru's Cordillera Azul — But the Forest Was Already Barely Shrinking

**Satellite analysis raises serious additionality questions over one of the Amazon's largest carbon credit schemes**

The headline number sounds impressive: 1,351,964 hectares of Peruvian rainforest, sheltered inside Cordillera Azul National Park, generating carbon credits that two of the world's largest fossil fuel companies have purchased to offset their emissions. But satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier tells a more complicated story — one in which the forest at the heart of VCS985 was losing trees at a fraction of the rate seen in surrounding unprotected land, raising profound questions about whether the credits represent real, additional climate benefit.

## A Forest That Was Already Holding Its Own

The project was registered on 22 December 2008. In the seven years before that date, according to Hansen Global Forest Change data analyzed by CarbonVerifier, the entire 1.36-million-hectare park lost just 1,777 hectares of forest — a remarkably low figure for a landscape of this scale in the western Amazon. In the fifteen years since the project started, total post-project loss stands at 8,345 hectares, or just 0.61% of the forest cover recorded in 2000.

That sounds like success. Walk through the year-by-year data, however, and a more uneven picture emerges.

## The Timeline: Spikes Amid Stability

For the first several years after registration, annual losses remained modest. In 2010, only 66 hectares were lost across the entire park — an area smaller than a small village. But 2013 brought a sharp spike: 1,311 hectares cleared in a single year, the highest annual loss recorded in the dataset to that point. Then 2015 surpassed even that, with 1,634 hectares lost — nearly double the cumulative loss of the entire pre-project period. A second major spike arrived in 2019, when 1,087 hectares disappeared.

These spikes are not trivial. They account for the bulk of all post-project deforestation and suggest that whatever protection the project provides has not been consistent. After 2019, losses contracted sharply, falling to 450 hectares in 2021, 256 in 2022, and 263 in 2023.

The NDVI vegetation index data from Sentinel-2 imagery, also analyzed by CarbonVerifier, offers a partial counterpoint. Mean NDVI rose from 0.641 in 2018 to a high of 0.831 in 2022, with the forest-classified fraction of the park climbing from 92.2% to 99.4% over the same period — and holding at 99.3% in 2024. The deforested fraction fell from 1.1% in 2018 to effectively zero by 2024. On this measure, the forest looks remarkably intact.

## The Additionality Problem

Here the evidence becomes most troubling. The core logic of REDD carbon credits depends on additionality: the forest would have been destroyed *but for* the project. CarbonVerifier's additionality test compares the park's post-start loss rate of 0.61% against four comparable unprotected control areas at the same latitude band in Peru.

The results are stark. Control Area 0 lost 3.08% of its 2000 forest cover. Control Area 2 lost 7.17%. Control Area 4 lost 3.02%. The mean control-area loss rate was 3.86 percentage points — more than six times the rate observed inside the park. The gap between the project and the controls is 3.24 percentage points.

CarbonVerifier's verdict on this gap is, nonetheless, "WEAK." The reason: the park was already a nationally protected area before the carbon project was layered on top of it. The lower deforestation rate may reflect existing legal protections rather than anything the REDD project specifically added. Satellite data cannot distinguish between the two.

## What This Means for the Carbon Market

Shell and TotalEnergies — the only buyers identified in the project's registry metadata — have purchased credits underpinned by this analysis. Both companies have publicly cited nature-based offset projects as components of their net-zero strategies.

The satellite record for VCS985 does not show a forest being razed and saved. It shows a largely intact forest that was already losing trees at minimal rates before a single credit was issued, in a park that already had legal protection. Whether the carbon market is pricing genuine climate benefit — or simply attaching a dollar value to a forest that was never seriously at risk — is a question the data alone cannot fully answer. But it is a question that buyers, regulators, and the Verra registry will need to address.

The voluntary carbon market has long promised that credits like these represent real, measurable, additional tonnes of CO₂ kept out of the atmosphere. For VCS985, the satellites suggest the reality is considerably more ambiguous.
